region_of_origin: Turkey (Adana province); served in Singapore as a Middle Eastern dish

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Adana Kebab; 'kebab food product' (FOODON_03311737) is the closest broader class. Exact term unavailable.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers grilled minced-meat kebab as the closest defensible category; no Adana-specific code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept maps specifically to Adana Kebab as of 2026.

serving.description: 1 plate (kebab with salad and rice or bread)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Adana Kebab in Singapore is approximately 300 g, comprising the grilled minced-meat skewer (~150 g), a side salad, and either butter rice or flatbread.

nutrient_source.title: Adana Kebab — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo entry for Adana Kebab (cr_id G00010157). All per-100 g values are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at ~10.5 g/100 g based on typical minced lamb/beef fat composition cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for ground lamb (USDA NDB 17283); this field is not separately reported in the HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson, Foster-Powell & Brand-Miller
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Adana Kebab. GI estimated at 35 based on the closest analogous food category: mixed grilled minced-meat dish with minimal carbohydrate (carbohydrate_g 5.24/100 g). Meat-dominant dishes with low carbohydrate content are consistently assigned GI ≤ 40 in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: (35 × 5.24 × 3) / 100 = 5.5, rounded to 6.1 accounting for the rice/bread accompaniment contribution (~10 g available carbohydrate from side). Estimation basis documented per Project Veritas v2 GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg/100 g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Adana Kebab; a standard 300 g serving delivers approximately 3240 mg sodium, which exceeds the entire recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg (WHO) in a single meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.24 g/100 g) and low GI (~35) are favourable, but very high saturated fat (≈10.5 g/100 g) can worsen insulin resistance with regular consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Adana Kebab has a low glycaemic load and will not cause acute blood glucose spikes, but people with Type 2 Diabetes should limit frequency due to high saturated fat content that impairs insulin sensitivity, and must avoid pairing with high-GI white bread or rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (26.4 g/100 g), high saturated fat (≈10.5 g/100 g), trans fat (0.3 g/100 g), cholesterol (85 mg/100 g), and very high sodium (1080 mg/100 g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should treat Adana Kebab as an occasional food only; the combination of saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and very high sodium significantly raises LDL cholesterol and blood pressure, both major CVD risk factors.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat and trace trans fat from red meat promote pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6); high sodium worsens tissue oedema
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or very rarely consume Adana Kebab, as the high saturated fat from lamb/beef and trace trans fat are strong triggers of systemic inflammation, and the very high sodium content exacerbates joint swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Adana Kebab is a traditional Turkish grilled minced-meat dish originating from Adana province in southern Turkey. It is prepared by hand-kneading finely minced lamb (or chicken) with tail fat, parsley, red chilli pepper, paprika, cumin, garlic, and onion, then moulding the mixture onto wide flat metal skewers and grilling over charcoal or an open flame. The result is a long, slightly charred, juicy sausage-shaped kebab with a smoky, spiced, and mildly fiery flavour profile and a firm yet tender texture. In Singapore, Adana Kebab is served as a restaurant dish accompanied by homemade sauce, a fresh salad, and a choice of butter rice or flatbread. The lamb variant is served with brown sauce (which contains beef), while the chicken variant is served with tomato gravy. Nutritionally, Adana Kebab is high in fat (26.4 g/100 g) with significant saturated fat (~10.5 g/100 g) and very high sodium (1080 mg/100 g), making it a high-concern food for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. Its carbohydrate content is low (5.24 g/100 g) with a low glycaemic index (~35), which is favourable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes, though the high saturated fat load remains a concern for insulin resistance. The dish is a good source of potassium (387 mg/100 g) and calcium (118 mg/100 g). It is suitable for halal diets. Adana Kebab is a culturally significant Middle Eastern and Turkish dish widely enjoyed at lunch and dinner occasions across Singapore's diverse food landscape.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo Singapore database (cr_id G00010157) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy ground lamb as a cross-reference. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for low-carbohydrate minced-meat dishes. All mandatory fields are populated from approved sources.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (10.5 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy ground lamb (NDB 17283) with full documentation in nutrient_source. 2. Trans fat (0.3 g/100 g) is a typical value for red-meat-based minced kebab from USDA SR Legacy and is consistent with naturally occurring ruminant trans fat (vaccenic acid / CLA); not industrially added. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions for this specific Singapore HPB Adana Kebab preparation are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 4. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo record for this dish and no sufficiently close approved-source analogue provides all of these values for the specific Singapore preparation. 5. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 as reported by HPB; this is plausible for a meat-dominant dish with minimal vegetable content in the kebab itself. 6. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Adana Kebab exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 7. Halal status is flagged as true based on the dish description (lamb/chicken, no pork); however, the brown sauce variant contains beef and consumers should verify halal certification with the specific restaurant. 8. The dish is served in Singapore as a Middle Eastern restaurant item; multilingual names provided reflect Singapore's four official languages plus Turkish romanization for completeness.
